title: Ten minutes of Portuguese every morning
summary: Explain how a tiny daily habit of ten minutes added up over a year into something you could not have planned in one day.
topic: How a small daily habit grows over time
prompt: Write 9–11 sentences explaining a small habit you do every day (ten minutes of a language, one page of a book, one short walk) and how it added up over time. Explain the routine and the result. Use present simple, time markers (every day, after, then), and comparatives.
hintStarters: Every morning, before I check my phone, I spend ten minutes…
At first it felt too small to matter, but after a few weeks…
I do not study for hours; I only do a little, but…
A year later, I can now understand things that used to…

hintVocab.meaningVi: thói quen hằng ngày, lề thói
theo dõi, ghi lại tiến độ

sampleText: Every morning, before I check my phone, I spend ten minutes learning Portuguese. I do not study for hours, and I do not go to a class. I just open an app, review ten words, and read one short sentence aloud. Then I close the app and start my day. At first it felt too small to matter. Ten minutes is shorter than the time I spend making coffee. Some mornings I almost skipped it, but I kept going because the habit was easy. After a few weeks, I noticed I could remember words without trying. After a few months, I could read a simple menu in Portuguese. I am not fluent, and I still make many mistakes, but I am much better than I was a year ago. The secret was not talent; it was being consistent. A little practice every day is stronger than a long study once a month, because you forget less between sessions. I keep a small calendar and mark each day I practise. The long line of marks makes me want to keep the habit. People often want big results fast, but most real progress is slow and quiet. Ten minutes seems like nothing, yet it built something I could not have planned in a single day.

sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: trước khi tôi xem điện thoại

sampleAnnotations.phrase: I spend ten minutes learning Portuguese
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: tôi dành mười phút học tiếng Bồ Đào Nha

sampleAnnotations.phrase: I do not study for hours
it felt too small to matter
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: nó có vẻ quá nhỏ để có ý nghĩa

sampleAnnotations.phrase: I could remember words without trying
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: tôi có thể nhớ từ mà không cần cố gắng

sampleAnnotations.phrase: I am much better than I was a year ago
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: tôi giỏi hơn nhiều so với một năm trước

sampleAnnotations.phrase: A little practice every day is stronger than a long study once a month
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: luyện tập một chút mỗi ngày mạnh hơn một buổi học dài mỗi tháng một lần
bạn quên ít hơn giữa các buổi
đánh dấu mỗi ngày tôi luyện tập

sampleAnnotations.phrase: most real progress is slow and quiet
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: phần lớn sự tiến bộ thật sự đều chậm và lặng lẽ
mười phút có vẻ như chẳng là gì

sampleAnnotations.phrase: I could not have planned in a single day
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: tôi không thể lên kế hoạch trong một ngày duy nhất

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Comparatives for change over time
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: Bài dùng so sánh hơn để cho thấy sự thay đổi: 'I am much better than I was a year ago' và 'A little practice every day is stronger than a long study'. 'Much' đứng trước so sánh hơn để nhấn mạnh mức độ.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: The text uses comparatives to show change: 'I am much better than I was a year ago' and 'A little practice every day is stronger than a long study'. 'Much' before a comparative makes the difference stronger.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: used to / could for past states and ability
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: Câu 'words that used to feel hard' và 'I could read a simple menu' dùng 'used to' cho trạng thái quá khứ không còn nữa, và 'could' cho khả năng đạt được. Lưu ý 'I could not have planned' nói về điều không thể trong quá khứ.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: Phrases like 'words that used to feel hard' and 'I could read a simple menu' use 'used to' for a past state and 'could' for ability. Note 'I could not have planned' talks about something impossible in the past.

sampleTranslationVi: Mỗi sáng, trước khi xem điện thoại, tôi dành mười phút học tiếng Bồ Đào Nha. Tôi không học hàng giờ, và tôi không đi học ở lớp. Tôi chỉ mở một ứng dụng, ôn lại mười từ, và đọc to một câu ngắn. Rồi tôi đóng ứng dụng và bắt đầu một ngày. Lúc đầu nó có vẻ quá nhỏ để có ý nghĩa. Mười phút ngắn hơn thời gian tôi pha cà phê. Vài buổi sáng tôi suýt bỏ qua nó, nhưng tôi vẫn tiếp tục vì thói quen này dễ. Sau vài tuần, tôi nhận ra mình có thể nhớ từ mà không cần cố gắng. Sau vài tháng, tôi có thể đọc một thực đơn đơn giản bằng tiếng Bồ Đào Nha. Tôi chưa lưu loát, và tôi vẫn mắc nhiều lỗi, nhưng tôi giỏi hơn nhiều so với một năm trước. Bí quyết không phải là tài năng; đó là sự đều đặn. Luyện tập một chút mỗi ngày mạnh hơn một buổi học dài mỗi tháng một lần, vì bạn quên ít hơn giữa các buổi. Tôi giữ một cuốn lịch nhỏ và đánh dấu mỗi ngày tôi luyện tập. Hàng dấu dài khiến tôi muốn giữ thói quen. Người ta thường muốn kết quả lớn nhanh chóng, nhưng phần lớn sự tiến bộ thật sự đều chậm và lặng lẽ. Mười phút có vẻ như chẳng là gì, nhưng nó đã xây nên điều mà tôi không thể lên kế hoạch trong một ngày duy nhất.

mcQuestions.prompt: Choose the best-written sentence about the daily routine.
options: Every morning I spend ten minutes to learn Portuguese.
Every morning I spend ten minutes for learning Portuguese.
Every morning I spend ten minutes learn Portuguese.
Every morning I spend ten minutes learning Portuguese.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Spend time + verb-ing' is the correct pattern: 'spend ten minutes learning'. 'To learn' and 'for learning' are wrong after 'spend time'; 'learn' lacks -ing.
mcQuestions.hint: After 'spend ten minutes', which verb form is correct?

mcQuestions.prompt: Pick the sentence with a correct comparative.
options: I am much better than I was a year ago.
I am much more better than I was a year ago.
I am much gooder than I was a year ago.
I am much better as I was a year ago.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Better than' is the correct comparative of 'good'. 'More better' is a double comparative; 'gooder' is not a word; 'as' is wrong with a comparative.
mcQuestions.hint: What is the comparative of 'good', and which word follows it?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence explains the benefit of daily practice correctly?
options: A little practice every day is more strong than a long study once a month.
A little practice every day is stronger than a long study once a month.
A little practice every day is strongest than a long study once a month.
A little practice every day is stronger that a long study once a month.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Stronger than' is the correct comparative form. 'More strong' is wrong; 'strongest' is superlative; 'that' should be 'than'.
mcQuestions.hint: Is the comparative of 'strong' one word or two?

mcQuestions.prompt: Choose the sentence that uses 'used to' correctly for a past state.
options: I can read words that use to feel hard.
I can read words that used to feeling hard.
I can read words that used to feel hard.
I can read words that are used to feel hard.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Used to + base verb' (feel) describes a past habit or state. 'Use to' drops the -d; 'feeling' is wrong after 'used to'; 'are used to' changes the meaning.
mcQuestions.hint: What verb form follows 'used to' for a past state?

mcQuestions.prompt: Pick the most natural sentence about almost stopping.
options: Some mornings I almost skip it but.
Some mornings I skipped it almost.
Some mornings I almost skipping it.
Some mornings I almost skipped it.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'I almost skipped it' places 'almost' before the verb naturally. The dangling 'but', misplaced 'almost', and 'skipping' without an auxiliary are all wrong.
mcQuestions.hint: Where does 'almost' usually go in relation to the verb?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly says she is not fully skilled yet?
options: I am not fluent, and I still make many mistakes.
I am not fluent, and I still do many mistakes.
I am not fluently, and I still make many mistakes.
I am not fluent, and I still making many mistakes.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Make mistakes' is the correct collocation and 'fluent' is the adjective. 'Do mistakes' is wrong; 'fluently' is an adverb; 'making' lacks an auxiliary.
mcQuestions.hint: Do we 'make' or 'do' mistakes in English?

mcQuestions.prompt: Choose the sentence that best states the real reason for success.
options: The secret was not talent; it was be consistent.
The secret was not talent; it was being consistent.
The secret was not talent; it was consistently.
The secret was not talent; it was being consistence.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Being consistent' (gerund + adjective) fits after 'it was'. 'Be consistent' lacks a gerund; 'consistently' is an adverb; 'consistence' is not the right noun here.

mcQuestions.prompt: Pick the sentence that explains why daily practice works, using 'because'.
options: It works because of you forget less between sessions.
It works because you forget fewer between sessions.
It works because you forget less between sessions.
It works, because you forgetting less between sessions.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Because you forget less' is correct; 'less' fits the uncountable idea here. 'Because of' needs a noun; 'fewer' needs a countable noun; 'forgetting' lacks an auxiliary.
mcQuestions.hint: Does 'because' join to a clause or to a noun?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence describes the small size of the habit naturally?
options: Ten minutes seem like nothing.
Ten minutes seems like nothings.
Ten minutes is seem like nothing.
Ten minutes seems like nothing.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Ten minutes' is treated as a single amount, so 'seems' is correct. 'Seem' disagrees; 'nothings' is wrong; 'is seem' is ungrammatical.
mcQuestions.hint: Is 'ten minutes' here one quantity or many separate things?

mcQuestions.prompt: Choose the best sentence about marking progress on a calendar.
options: I mark each day I practise on a small calendar.
I mark each day I practise in a small calendar.
I mark every days I practise on a small calendar.
I marking each day I practise on a small calendar.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Each day' (singular) and 'on a calendar' are correct. 'In a calendar' is unnatural; 'every days' is ungrammatical; 'I marking' lacks an auxiliary.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Why do small daily habits often feel pointless at the start, even though they may matter more than big one-time efforts? How can someone stay patient through the slow part?
